summary: I was born in Japan in 1899, specifically in Wakayama Prefecture. My early life there was in a quiet village, and I attended elementary school and later Meisei Business School in Osaka. Japan was where my family roots were, and it was also where my father returned after his first trip abroad before coming back to the United States. I remember some of the historical events like the Russo-Japanese War during my childhood in Japan.

I come from Wakayama Prefecture in Japan, a quiet village where I grew up with my brothers and sisters. My father was a shrine carpenter there, and my family had some connection to cargo boats from Osaka. The village was small with limited job opportunities, which was part of the reason my father sought work abroad. Many people from our village eventually formed the Tawara Villagers' Association here in the United States.

My father first went to Australia before coming to the United States. He waited there for a chance to get a job on an American military boat. Australia was a stepping stone for him before he reached San Francisco, where he eventually landed and started working. I don't have many details about his time in Australia, but it was part of his journey to the U.S.

San Francisco was the place where my father finally landed after coming from Australia. He swam from the military boat anchored offshore to the pier and found his way to the Japanese community on Fourth Street. He worked for the railroad company there as a repairman and even recuperated at San Pedro Hospital for beriberi. San Francisco was the first U.S. city my father settled in before returning to Japan and then coming back again.

My father was treated at San Pedro Hospital in San Francisco when he suffered from beriberi. He stayed there for a while to recuperate before returning to Japan. I know this because my family talked about his illness and recovery during his early years in the United States. San Pedro Hospital was an important place for his health during that difficult time.

I attended Meisei Business School in Osaka, which was a Catholic school. My mother's family had business ties connected to cargo boats from Osaka, like the Tenyo-Maru. Osaka was a significant city for my education and family business background. Also, my mother's younger brother went to a pharmacy school in Osaka before he passed away.

Kaiyo-Maru was one of the cargo boats connected to my mother's family business in Japan. Although I was more familiar with the Tenyo-Maru, I knew about Kaiyo-Maru when I was little. These boats transported cargo between Osaka and other places, playing a role in my family's livelihood back home. They represented the kind of business my mother's family was involved in before we moved abroad.

The Tenyo-Maru was a cargo ship that my mother's family owned or operated in Japan. It sailed routes involving Osaka and was part of the family's business activities. I remember hearing about it when I was young, and it symbolized the kind of transport business my family was involved in before I came to the United States. It was a significant part of my family's history in Japan.

I attended Meisei Business School in Osaka, which was a Catholic institution. I chose to go there because I was interested in business, influenced by my mother's business-minded nature. However, my father stopped sending money for my tuition, and my elder brother encouraged me to come to the United States instead. Meisei Business School was where I nurtured my early ambitions before leaving Japan.

The Meisei Business School I attended in Osaka was a Catholic school, so I was exposed to the Catholic Church through my education. Although I didn't speak much about religion, the school's affiliation meant that Catholic teachings were part of the environment. It was part of my school experience in Japan before I came to the United States.

The Tawara Villagers' Association was formed here in the United States by people from my home village in Wakayama Prefecture. Almost every family from the village had at least one person who came to the U.S., and the association helped maintain connections among us. It was a way to keep our community ties alive despite being far from home. I was aware of this group as part of the immigrant experience of my village people.

I was asked about the Sino-Japanese War, but I think the Russo-Japanese War happened when I was little. I don't recall much about the Sino-Japanese War itself. The wars were part of the historical backdrop of my early years in Japan, but I was too young to have clear memories of them.

The Russo-Japanese War took place when I was a child, and I remember participating in a lantern procession during that time. It was one of the significant events during my early years in Japan. Although I was young, the war was part of the atmosphere in my village and country. It left an impression on me as a notable historical event from my childhood.

When I came to the United States at age sixteen in 1916, I stopped overnight in Hawaii. I stayed at a Japanese-run hotel, which was modest but welcoming. There was a misunderstanding where some people thought I was an alien or a thief, but once they realized I spoke Japanese, they understood. Hawaii was a brief but memorable stop on my journey to the mainland U.S.

I boarded the ship called Shugyo-Maru at Kobe when I left Japan for the United States. Kobe was the port city where my journey began, and I remember buying fruit there before boarding. It was the starting point of my long voyage across the Pacific. Kobe was important as the place where I left Japan to join my family in America.

The Shugyo-Maru was the ship I took from Kobe to Yokohama on my way to the United States. During the trip, I experienced some incidents, like having my fruit stolen on board and noticing changes to the ship's entrances. The ship also had a shop where passengers could buy goods, though it wasn't compulsory. Shugyo-Maru was part of my first journey abroad as a young man.

After boarding at Kobe, the ship arrived at Yokohama, where I left my belongings on board and went to Tokyo to visit a relative. Yokohama was a key port city in my journey to the United States. Although I got lost in Tokyo and couldn't meet my relative, I returned to Yokohama to continue my voyage. Yokohama was an important stop before I finally left Japan.

I went to Tokyo from Yokohama to see a relative who was a senior at a pharmacy school. Unfortunately, I got lost and couldn't find him despite asking a policeman for directions. Tokyo was a big and confusing city for me at that time. After failing to meet my relative, I returned to Yokohama to continue my journey to the United States.

Although not explicitly detailed, Angel Island was likely part of the immigration process when I arrived in the United States. Many Japanese immigrants passed through Angel Island as they entered the country. It was part of the journey for people like me coming from Japan during that era. Angel Island represents the gateway for many immigrants seeking new lives in America.
